# A Course Kit for Teaching Loop Ownership {#sec-appendix-f-teaching-kit}

```{=latex}
\abstract*{@sec-teaching-the-loop argues that a rejection-bound field should train judgment over generation, and it notes that building the rubrics to grade that judgment is work the education community has barely begun. This appendix is a starting point for that work. It operationalizes the closing chapter into a set of assignments and a grading rubric a course can use directly. It reuses the design-loop card of @sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card as the unit of work and does not restate the reweighting of @tbl-teaching-reweight.}
```

@sec-teaching-the-loop makes the argument: when generation is cheap, the durable
skill is judgment, and the field should teach toward the part of the loop that is
scarce. This appendix is the practical companion to that chapter. It does not
repeat the case, and it does not reprint the design-loop card of
@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card. It gives an instructor the two things the
closing chapter deliberately left open: concrete assignments built on the card,
and a rubric that grades a defensible decision rather than a working artifact.

## Folding the Layer onto an Existing Course

The kit does not ask for a new course. The classical foundations still have to be
taught, because you cannot judge a design you do not understand, and the
reweighting in @tbl-teaching-reweight is a change of emphasis, not a
replacement. The single through-line is the principle that closes
@sec-teaching-the-loop, to teach the loop and not the tool: train architects to
govern loops and to reject with reason, not only to produce artifacts. In practice that means the design-loop card becomes the connective
instrument of the course. It is introduced once, then reused as the format for
paper discussions, project proposals, and design reviews, so that every artifact
a student produces exposes the loop behind it rather than only its result.

## Assignments

Each assignment below produces a completed design-loop card, so the card is both
the unit of submission and the unit of grading. They run from reading, to
proposing, to critiquing, in rising order of difficulty.

- **Paper to loop.** Take a recent AI-assisted architecture paper. Fill in the
  card for it, then choose one omitted loop field: represented state, legal
  actions, workload records, feedback budget, negative traces, rejection
  authority, or human owner, and state what record would have changed the claim.
  This teaches reading a loop before building one.
- **Proposal as a loop.** Before any implementation, require the completed card
  as the project proposal. Following @sec-teaching-the-loop, hand a student the
  lighthouse prompt of @sec-moonshot and a blank card; the task is not to produce
  a design but to expose the loop that would make one credible, and to state what
  could reject it. Grade the proposal on loop quality, not ambition.
- **Two papers, one commitment.** Give two papers that claim a similar result.
  Ask which result the student would commit to, argued only from loop fields such
  as exposed design space, feedback cost, evidence ledger, negative traces, and
  rejection authority, not from headline numbers alone; the numbers count only
  through their workload scope, provenance, fidelity, negative traces, and
  rejection authority.
- **Break the loop.** Give a described loop and ask the student to find the state
  it cannot observe or the proxy it optimizes incorrectly, then propose the
  rejection rule that would catch the failure. This is where a student learns
  that a fluent, confident result can still be wrong.

## A Rubric That Grades the Decision

@sec-teaching-the-loop observes that if the artifact is no longer the
achievement, then grading a working artifact measures the wrong thing, and that
agreeing on rubrics that grade judgment is barely-begun work. @tbl-teaching-rubric
is a starting point. Each dimension maps to a field on the design-loop card, and
each asks whether the student made a rejectable claim, not whether the artifact
ran.

| Dimension | Weak (low credit) | Strong (full credit) |
|---|---|---|
| Intent and constraints | Optimizes an available benchmark or proxy. | States a bounded objective, hard constraints, and non-goals, and names the decision owner. |
| Representation and exposed design space | Reports successful endpoints only. | Records assumptions, costs, failures, and negative traces, with provenance, and is explicit about what the loop cannot see. |
| Environment validity | Treats a tool as an oracle. | Defines legal actions, observations, invalid states, costs, provenance, and failure semantics for each tool result. |
| Evidence ledger | Cites a single score as proof. | States what cheap evidence can screen, what higher-fidelity feedback must confirm, and where the claim must stop or escalate. |
| Rejection rule | Names no way the result could be wrong. | Names an explicit rule, ideally checked by an independent authority, that would reject the result. |
| Commitment | Leaves no owner for the final decision. | Identifies the person who accepts the consequences of committing, and stops at the evidence level that supports it. |

: **Grade the decision, not the artifact:** Each dimension mirrors a field on the design-loop card and asks whether the loop was made explicit enough for another architect to reject or reuse it. {#tbl-teaching-rubric tbl-colwidths="[26,32,42]"}

The rubric is meant to be adapted, argued over, and shared, which is exactly the
community work @sec-teaching-the-loop calls for. The durable target it grades
toward is not a student who can generate a design, but one who can be trusted to
own the loop that decides whether a design is committed.
